reasoning:
The series tracks the 'Earnings Remittances Due to the U.S. Treasury', which acts as a deferred asset. Because the Fed has been operating at a loss, this series takes a negative value that decreases (becomes more negative) each week by roughly $500M-$1B. For the series to 'increase' compared to its June 8 level, it must become less negative. This would require the Federal Reserve to achieve positive net income and earn enough profit to dig out of any additional hole created between June 8 and its breakeven point. 

Given the current downward trend and Morgan Stanley's estimate that the Fed is only close to breaking even, it will likely take months to strictly enter positive net income, meaning the deferred asset will drop significantly lower before eventually turning around. Because of the math of having to erase freshly accumulated summer losses, it is highly likely that values on all listed dates remain lower than June 8. Unlikely positive accounting blips provide a small, constant baseline for YES in the short run. Probabilities scale up towards the end of the year as uncertainty roughly increases, accommodating scenarios where rapid and severe interest rate cuts occur, leading to very high unforecasted Fed profits.
